content.text: is a pattern where the model can call tools repeatedly, receive results, and continue reasoning — all within a single function call. The Vercel AI SDK implements this with the
is one model call. If the model's
, the SDK executes the requested tools, appends the results to the message history, and makes another model call — transparently, without any extra code in your application.

attrs.alt: AI SDK agentic loop: model call → tool-calls branch executes tools and loops back → stop branch returns final result; maxSteps guards against infinite loops

content.text: to enable multi-step agentic behaviour. Without it (default
), the SDK makes exactly one model call and returns — even if the model emits tool calls:
One entry per model call, with tool calls and results for each
Why the loop ended: 'stop', 'length', 'max-steps', 'tool-calls' (if no execute)
Accumulated token counts across all steps
array gives you full visibility into what happened in each iteration of the loop — the model's text, tool calls it made, results it received, and token usage:
Logging steps in development is the most effective way to understand why an agent took a particular path. In production, record step counts and total tokens per task to monitor agent efficiency.
(a subsequent call after tool execution). The loop always starts with
as long as the model keeps emitting tool calls.
When you need fine-grained control — token budget tracking, step-level logging, conditional termination, or dynamic tool addition — implement the loop manually instead of using
The manual loop appends each model response and tool result to the
array before the next call. This is exactly what
does internally; the manual version simply exposes every step.

Stop sequences are consumed by the model — they do not appear in the returned
to distinguish a clean stop (sequence found) from a length truncation.
for a streaming agentic loop. Text tokens stream progressively; tool calls execute silently between text segments. The
event type distinguishes text-delta from tool events:
For multi-session agents — chatbots, personal assistants — persist the message history to a database and reload it on each call. Pass the full history as the
Trim history when it grows too large. A common approach is to keep the last N messages, or to summarise older context with a separate LLM call before adding new messages.
Agentic loops can accumulate tokens quickly across many steps. Track totals to surface cost in dashboards and to detect runaway loops early:
Number of model calls in the loop (prevents infinite loops)
Tokens generated per individual model call (prevents huge single responses)
Ends generation on a specific marker — useful for task-completion signals
when tools need sequential execution. Without it, the SDK calls the model once — tool calls are emitted but the model never sees the results.
is exhausted the result may be incomplete. Either increase
or handle the partial result explicitly.
function returns the same result and the model always requests the same tool, the loop will run until
is exhausted. Guard tools against repetitive calls with state tracking.
Token cost: each step in an agentic loop is a separate API call. A 10-step loop costs at minimum 10 × the price of a single call, plus accumulated context that grows with each step.
Tool calls and multi-step loops introduce new failure modes. The AI SDK surfaces them as typed errors that you can catch and act on selectively.
in a try/catch and narrow the error type with the static
methods. This lets you recover from tool definition issues without silencing all errors.
— model invoked a tool name that was not registered
— tool arguments failed the Zod schema
— loop terminated at the maxSteps ceiling (not thrown by default, but available if you set
Large agentic tasks benefit from decomposition: a
agent classifies the request and delegates to specialist sub-agents, each with its own tool set.
This pattern keeps each agent small and testable. The classifier uses
for a typed enum output; the specialist uses
with domain-specific tools and a larger
Keep domain boundaries hard: a finance agent must not have access to calendar write tools. Tool access control is your primary safety surface in multi-agent pipelines.
Transient API failures and rate limits are common in agentic loops. The AI SDK lets you configure retries at the provider level and implement custom backoff in manual loops.
For streaming loops, implement retry at the stream consumption level: catch the stream error, reset the message history to the last good checkpoint, and restart the loop iteration.
Multi-step loops are harder to debug than single calls. The AI SDK provides structured step metadata and callback hooks for tracing.
